Paid Block Conversion Durable Fix Plan

Created: 2026-05-30 18:14 SAST

Goal: stop wasting ASIC hash power by making the stack prove that hash is becoming accepted, confirmed, paid on-chain blocks.

Executive Plan

This is not a pool tuning task. The durable fix is a cross-repo program that moves mining-template validity and submit readiness into corechain, makes the pool consume that contract instead of guessing from local age/current-job/current-parent state, and makes dashboard, watchdog, release, and canary gates judge success by confirmed paid block conversion per active ASIC MAC-hour.

Blocking rule: Do not build, ship, or deploy the current staged blockdag-corechain index until the staged regression is reviewed. The protocol review found staged changes that appear to remove recent-template submit replay, weaken zero-address rejection, remove default submit-obsolete-depth behavior, and stop comparing coinbase address in miner template equality.

Assumptions Audit

AssumptionStatusPlan Response
The ASIC private Ethernet/NAT link is the root cause.Rejected by RCA and reviewers.Keep router/DHCP hardening, but focus on template validity, submit readiness, local candidate drops, and chain confirmation.
Chain synced means mining ready.False.Add first-class paid mining state. Require template source readiness, accepted submits, or on-chain confirmation.
Accepted shares prove earning.False.Shares are connectivity and ASIC-work telemetry only. Paid health uses accepted submits and confirmed blue/payout-matched chain blocks.
A hard sub-second candidate age is safer on a roughly 1 second chain.Unproven and currently harmful.Pool wall-clock expiry becomes a backstop only. Corechain validity/expiry is authoritative.
Current parent mismatch always means unpaid.Unsafe.Corechain recent-template replay and obsolete-depth rules decide whether an old template remains submit-valid.
Dashboard overall ok is enough.False.Dashboard exposes separate paid_mining_state and blocks green mining status when paid conversion is degraded.
OfficePool is a good first development lab.False.Use source tests, offline replay, and a non-mining test host first. OfficePool is a late guarded canary.

Reviewer Synthesis

ReviewerMain CritiqueIntegrated Requirement
R-Daneel, corechain/protocolSeq is not a durable work identity; current/staged corechain can reject valid old solved headers after a newer template.Add template_id, immutable template cache, SubmitBlockHeaderV3, explicit submit-valid outcomes, and restore paid-conversion protections before new work.
R-Giskard, pool/StratumThe pool currently discards paid work using local age, current-job, parent, and sequence heuristics.Network-target candidates must not be locally dropped unless corechain says the template is invalid or the hash is objectively duplicate.
R-Calvin, SRE/release/QAThe fix will regress again unless releases are gated by paid conversion, pinned manifests, canary windows, and rollback rehearsal.Add a paid-conversion harness and promotion gates based on confirmed-blue paid blocks per active MAC/X100 miner-hour.
R-Susan, dashboard/operationsPaid mining truth is currently a derived warning; fresh shares can still soften real unpaid mining states.Add top-level paid_mining_state, split share activity from paid submit activity, and make watchdog repairs use paid evidence.

Target Contract

The final system has one explicit contract: corechain owns mining work validity; the pool transports work and submits candidates; dashboard and release gates verify paid outcomes.

Corechain Exposes

  • template_id: stable identity for a built mining template, separate from stream sequence.
  • template_seq: stream/order sequence only, not work identity.
  • issued_at_unix_ms, valid_until_unix_ms or valid_for_submit_ms when authoritative.
  • template_parent_root, parent set hash, height/order context, tx root, state root, coinbase address, pow type, version.
  • mineable_now, submit_ready, template_cached_usable, last_good_template_valid, submit_replay_available.
  • invalidation_seq, invalidation_cause, submit_obsolete_height_limit, and structured reason codes.

Pool Consumes

  • Each JobData stores corechain template_id, backend identity, validity fields, parent root, and issue time.
  • Block candidates submit with template_id to the issuing backend using V3 when available.
  • Local drops for expired, stale-parent, or non_current_job are forbidden for network-target candidates while corechain says the template is submit-valid.
  • When no valid template source exists, the pool pauses new work rather than feeding miners guessed work.

Operations Verifies

  • Paid health is based on accepted block submits and confirmed on-chain/blue/payout-matched blocks.
  • Shares are shown as ASIC connectivity and work telemetry only.
  • Release promotion fails if paid conversion is stale or unverifiable, even when containers are running and shares are flowing.

Step By Step Execution Plan

Phase 0: Freeze, Quarantine, And Baseline

  1. Quarantine the staged blockdag-corechain changes. Do not build from that index. Review and either restore or explicitly justify any change affecting recent-template replay, zero-address rejection, coinbase comparison, and submit obsolete depth.
  2. Create or link child SDLC issues under backlog item PVTI_lADODbdoIs4BXjCZzguQO_E for corechain, pool, dashboard, stack/release, and validation harness.
  3. Capture a read-only OfficePool baseline: image digests, source SHAs, env, .env, systemd units, chain path, miner MACs, pool metrics, node health, dashboard JSON, and chain confirmation sample.
  4. Record current paid-conversion baseline: network-target candidates, accepted submits, local drops, tip-overdue, invalidated/non-current/stale rejects, confirmed-blue/payout-matched blocks, active MAC miner-hours.
  5. Define rollback points for pool-only and corechain changes before any live canary.

Exit criteria: no one can accidentally deploy the staged corechain regression; baseline and rollback records exist; issue structure exists.

Phase 1: Build The Paid-Conversion Evidence Harness

  1. Add a candidate timeline collector that joins pool logs/DB, Prometheus metrics, node RPC, chain headers, and miner MAC identity.
  2. For every network-target candidate, record: ASIC MAC, worker, job ID, template ID/seq if known, backend, template age, parent/root, receive time, local decision, submit RPC result, block hash/order, coinbase/payout address, blue/red status, settlement age.
  3. Support offline replay from captured OfficePool logs and live observe-only sampling without changing pool behavior.
  4. Emit pass/fail output, not only plots. Include exact reasons for unverified or unpaid candidates.
  5. Add dashboard/API exposure for the latest harness result and raw evidence path.

Exit criteria: the harness can reproduce the current failure baseline and show which candidates were dropped locally, rejected by node, accepted but unconfirmed, or confirmed paid.

Phase 2: Corechain Owns Template Validity

  1. Restore paid-conversion protections: recent-template submit replay, fail-closed zero-address template rejection in all mining modes, coinbase address comparison in miner template equality, and default submit-obsolete-depth behavior/tests.
  2. Add durable template identity fields to HTTP getBlockTemplate, WebSocket RemoteGBTResult, mining template health, and submit result structs. Add fields only; do not break legacy clients.
  3. Implement an immutable template registry in services/miner, keyed by template_id, bounded by count and TTL, with hit/miss/eviction/expired/accepted-from-cache metrics.
  4. Add SubmitBlockHeaderV3(headerHex, template_id, extraNonce). V3 reconstructs from the cached immutable template when valid instead of the current live template.
  5. Return structured outcomes: accepted, template_id_unknown, template_expired, submit_obsolete_depth, coinbase_mismatch, txroot_mismatch, node_syncing, template_not_submit_ready, and transport errors.
  6. Keep V2 backward compatible and preserve recent-template replay for legacy matching during rollout.
  7. Tighten mining health semantics: separate transport liveness, template cached usable, last-good valid, mineable now, submit ready, submit replay available, and build error blocking.
  8. Preserve latest-only template scheduling: parent/tip and submit-obsolete invalidations outrank mempool churn; mempool rebuild bursts coalesce; slow WebSocket subscribers cannot block emission.
  9. Make BdagPool.ResetTemplate() failures explicit and non-destructive: failed rebuilds must not replace a still-good pending environment.

Corechain tests: old solved header submits after newer template; node restart returns template_id_unknown; zero address is blocked in all modes; coinbase mismatch fails explicitly; obsolete depth is honored; last-good survives nonblocking EVM pending failure but expires on parent mismatch/true expiry; slow subscriber does not block template stream.

Phase 3: Pool Consumes The Contract In Observe-Only Mode

  1. Add new template validity fields to pool template models and JobData.
  2. Feature-detect V3 submit support per backend. Keep legacy behavior as default for the first observe-only build.
  3. Store corechain validity fields with each job and emit metrics without changing local block-candidate decisions.
  4. Record what the new policy would have done for each local drop: would submit, would reject with source-invalid, would clean-refresh, or would pause work.
  5. Split source states: transport healthy, template source eligible, submit eligible, paid-mining eligible. Do not let fresh template transport alone imply submit readiness.
  6. Harden runtime admin: loopback-only by default or action-token required; every mutation writes an audit record and rollback values.

Exit criteria: observe-only data proves which current local drops are false drops under the corechain contract, without increasing risk on live mining.

Phase 4: Pool Enforces The Contract

  1. Add flags: POOL_TEMPLATE_VALIDITY_CONTRACT_ENABLED, POOL_BLOCK_CANDIDATE_POLICY=legacy|observe|contract, and emergency fallback to legacy.
  2. Reorder mining.submit for known old jobs: if the job is retained, reconstruct and hash before rejecting it as non-current. Cheap stale-share rejects remain allowed for non-block shares.
  3. For network-target candidates, submit when corechain says the template is submit-valid, even if local age/current-job/current-parent heuristics would have rejected it.
  4. Reject locally only for objective duplicates, malformed work, or source-authoritative invalidity.
  5. Pin submit validation to the backend/template that issued the job. Cross-source submission must require an explicit compatible template contract.
  6. Replace broad clean-refresh triggers with source-classified invalidations. Mempool churn must not force clean work unless corechain says old work is no longer submit-valid.
  7. Demote adaptive optimizer until paid evidence exists. It may tune load/latency knobs, not candidate-age truth.

Pool tests: non-current but source-valid block candidate is submitted; stale share is rejected cheaply; source-invalid block candidate is dropped with reason; parent-changed valid-within-depth submits; mempool-only churn does not invalidate paid work; submit-ready false with valid last-good serves; submit-ready false with no valid template pauses work; runtime admin auth works; legacy fallback works.

Phase 5: Dashboard And Watchdog Make Paid Mining First Class

  1. Add top-level paid_mining_state: sync_only_no_miners, ready_no_miners, mining_paid_ok, mining_paid_degraded, mining_unpaid, template_source_unready, stack_down.
  2. Split current mixed health booleans into has_recent_share_activity, has_recent_block_candidate_activity, has_recent_accepted_submit, and has_recent_confirmed_onchain_paid_block.
  3. Add a first-screen paid-mining panel: state, last accepted submit age, last on-chain paid block, candidate acceptance ratio, main loss reason, selected backend mineable/submit-ready/template age, current repair action, cooldown.
  4. Make watchdog suppression use accepted submit or chain confirmation, not shares.
  5. Detect hashing-but-unpaid explicitly. Repair order: runtime admin/template refresh, pool submit path, node/template source health, then ASIC only if ASIC API/hash health is independently bad.
  6. Persist incidents when accepted shares continue but paid submits do not. Repeated failed repairs mark dashboard critical.
  7. Move miner repair cooldowns and identity state to MAC keys, not IPs.

Dashboard tests: fresh shares plus stale accepted submits and node_submit_ready=false produces paid-mining degraded/unpaid; fresh accepted submits without chain confirmation is pending verification, not fully paid ok; no miners shows sync-only and skips template probes.

Phase 6: Stack Profiles, Installers, And Release Packaging

  1. Add explicit capability profiles, including pi5-asic-router-usb, Pi5 non-router, AMD64 SSD/NVMe, ARM64 SSD/NVMe, no-miner sync node, multi-ASIC pool, and source-serving eligibility.
  2. Expose profile facts in /api/status: one production node, DHCP/NAT gateway, USB chain data, template backend count, miner identity mode, IO budget, source-serving eligibility.
  3. On USB/router profiles, do not enable source-serving timers that stage, publish, rsync, tar, scan, or serve chain artifacts.
  4. Preserve no-miner sync-only behavior in installer and repair paths: no mining flags, no template probes, no mining work generation until actual miner demand and a non-zero payment address exist.
  5. Release manifest pins exact SHAs and image digests for pool, blockdag-corechain, dashboard, and pool-stack-docker.
  6. Runtime status exposes packaged repo SHAs/image digests and validates they match the release manifest. Mining start refuses on mismatch unless a logged emergency override is set.
  7. Keep repo boundaries clean: dashboard source in dashboard; stack packages/pins it and validates packaged copy.

Exit criteria: USB/router hosts cannot accidentally become artifact sources; fresh installs remain sync-only without miners; releases are provenance-pinned and drift-detecting.

Phase 7: Verification And Canary Gates

  1. Stage 0: unit and integration tests pass on Linux ARM64 and AMD64. Include corechain, pool, dashboard, stack packaging, and release architecture tests.
  2. Stage 1: offline replay of captured OfficePool failure data shows reduced false local expiry and no increase in stale-parent or unsafe submits.
  3. Stage 2: non-mining test host validates sync, template health, profile detection, release manifest, and dashboard paid state without ASICs.
  4. Stage 3: observe-only one-X100 canary, minimum 60 minutes for pool-only changes and 4 hours for corechain/template changes.
  5. Stage 4: active one-X100 canary with rollback armed. Compare to baseline and comparable X100 normalized by active MAC miner-hour.
  6. Stage 5: multi-miner canary only after single-X100 passes. Never use multi-miner as the first proof point.
  7. Stage 6: 12 to 24 hour live Pi5 appliance soak before replacing a known-good release.

Initial SLOs: node mineable and submit-ready for at least 95 percent of active window excluding declared maintenance; local block-candidate discard under 5 percent unless source-authoritative; tip-overdue under 3 to 5 percent during early canary; paid confirmed-blue blocks per active X100/MAC-hour no worse than 90 percent of comparable control, then tighten after confidence improves.

Phase 8: Rollback And Drift Prevention

  1. Before each canary, capture rollback: image IDs, source SHAs, compose env, .env, systemd state, database migration state, chain path, dashboard/runtime config, and watchdog overrides.
  2. Pool rollback first when only pool behavior regresses. Corechain rollback only if structured validity or submit replay is proven wrong or node stability regresses.
  3. Watchdogs must not fight rollback or automatically re-upgrade.
  4. Runtime admin overrides must be reversible by clearing overrides and restoring persisted env.
  5. CI and release scripts fail if paid-conversion gates are missing, stale, or not tied to the exact release manifest.
  6. Memory and SDLC item must receive canary evidence before promotion.

Repository Work Breakdown

RepoOwned WorkDo Not Merge Until
blockdag-corechainTemplate identity, immutable cache, V3 submit, structured outcomes, latest-only/last-good semantics, nodeworker readiness gate, zero-address and coinbase invariants.Regression quarantine resolved; corechain tests prove old valid solved headers are accepted after template refresh.
poolStore template contract in jobs, V3 submit support, protected network-target candidate path, router eligibility split, contract-mode metrics, runtime admin hardening.Corechain contract exists and observe-only pool data confirms false local drops.
dashboardPaid mining state, split recent activity truths, first-screen paid panel, watchdog paid-conversion repairs, MAC-keyed miner state, runbooks.Tests prove shares cannot mask unpaid mining.
pool-stack-dockerCapability profiles, USB low-IO source blocking, no-miner sync-only installer enforcement, release manifest/pinning, canary and rollback scripts, packaging validation.Release gate can prove exact source/image provenance and paid-conversion evidence.

Final Definition Of Done

  • Corechain is the authority for template validity and submit readiness.
  • The pool does not discard network-target candidates from local wall-clock/current-job guesses while the source says submit-valid.
  • The dashboard answers "am I earning?" on the first screen and cannot show paid mining green when submits or chain confirmation are stale.
  • Watchdog repairs unpaid mining as a paid-conversion incident, not as generic share or container health.
  • USB/router profile cannot serve bulk chain data by default.
  • No-miner installs remain sync-only forever by default until real miner demand and a non-zero payment address exist.
  • Release manifests pin all repos and images, and runtime exposes those pins.
  • Promotion requires confirmed-blue paid blocks per active MAC/X100 miner-hour over a meaningful canary window.
  • Rollback is rehearsed and cannot be undone by watchdog drift.
